locale_market_context.market_boundary: English reader-facing copy is written for US/UK/EU actuarial work context. It is not a translation of the Chinese asset and does not carry China-only regulatory examples.

summary: FermatMind rates actuaries at 8/10 for AI task exposure because actuarial work includes data reconciliation, probability modeling, scenario testing, report drafting, and model review. This is not a replacement prediction; the durable skill signal is defining risk boundaries, explaining model outputs, and owning assumptions.

facts.reader_safe_interpretation: High task exposure: AI can accelerate actuarial data checks, batch model runs, scenario drafts, and report preparation, while out-of-distribution risk judgment, model explainability, regulatory accountability, and cross-functional tradeoffs remain human responsibilities.

items.label: Data reconciliation and cleaning
items.interpretation: AI can help check premium, claims, lapse, mortality, accident, or loss data, generate SQL/Python cleaning scripts, map fields, and validate tables under human review.

items.label: Batch model runs and scenario drafts
items.interpretation: Within established actuarial systems and model frameworks, AI can accelerate life tables, loss reserves, cash-flow projections, sensitivity tests, stress-test outputs, and first-pass explanations.

items.label: Out-of-distribution risk judgment
items.interpretation: When historical data is thin, such as autonomous-vehicle liability, climate-related extreme loss, new health-risk patterns, or new insurance products, actuaries still need to set the initial assumption boundary.

items.label: Model explainability and accountability
items.interpretation: More complex nonlinear models are not automatically more usable. Actuaries still need to explain where a model may fail and whether its results can be accepted by regulators and business decision-makers.

items.label: Skill evidence to build
items.interpretation: The stronger signal is evidence of problem definition, model explanation, black-box review, and accountability-aware use of actuarial outputs, not only routine software operation.

sources.boundary: Used as task evidence, not as an AI-risk score.

sources.source_name: Pew Research Center AI and O*NET methodology
sources.used_for: External example of mapping AI exposure to work activities
sources.boundary: Methodology reference only; does not publish this mock occupation score.

task_evidence.task_label: Analyze statistical data to estimate risk and uncertainty
task_evidence.captured_fact: Actuarial work includes recurring quantitative analysis, modeling, and risk-estimation tasks.
task_evidence.boundary: Mock example based on occupation task structure; production rows must capture exact source snippets.
task_evidence.exposure_relevance: Quantitative information-processing tasks can be accelerated by AI tools under human review.

task_evidence.task_label: Prepare reports and communicate risk findings
task_evidence.captured_fact: The occupation includes documentation and communication of analytical findings.

task_evidence.exposure_relevance: Drafting, summarization, and consistency checking can be AI-assisted.

ai_exposure_score.rubric_source: FermatMind internal AI exposure rubric calibrated against task-level external AI exposure literature
ai_exposure_score.score_interpretation: A score of 8 indicates high task exposure to AI assistance or partial automation, not job-loss probability.

Production rows must capture exact O*NET task snippets and pass trust audit before asset generation.
